preFix.title: Replacing a tap washer in a dripping pillar tap
preFix.subtitle: Fix a dripping hot or cold pillar tap by renewing the seat washer
preFix.excerpt: How to fix a dripping pillar tap by turning off the water supply, dismantling the tap headgear, removing the worn jumper washer, fitting a new washer, and reassembling the tap.
preFix.sourceNotes: Collins Complete DIY Manual. Plumbing for Beginners (1918, Traps, Wastes and Overflows). Cassell's Cyclopaedia of Mechanics.

content.text: A pillar tap drips because the rubber
has worn flat. Pillar taps are the traditional separate hot and cold taps found on baths and kitchen sinks. Removing the
to reach the washer means turning off the water supply first. Unlike a modern cartridge tap, the washer is cheap and takes no specialist parts.

A replacement tap washer: standard size is 13 mm diameter (1/2 inch BSP). Buy a pack of assorted tap washers from a hardware shop rather than a single size, as the exact size may vary slightly between tap manufacturers. A tap reseating tool (optional) if the tap seat inside the tap body is scored or pitted. Silicone grease for the headgear threads on reassembly.
Step 1, turn off the supply and open the tap fully

Turn off the isolating valve on the supply pipe below the tap, or turn off the main stop tap. Open the tap fully to drain the residual pressure and any water in the pipe. Put the bucket under the tap. If the tap is still dripping slowly after opening fully, the supply is not fully shut: find the correct stop valve and close it.
Step 2, remove the tap cover and expose the headgear nut

Unscrew the decorative cover from the top of the tap: it either unscrews by hand or has a small screw under a plastic button on the top that holds it. Removing the cover reveals the large hexagonal headgear nut at the top of the tap body. Pad the jaws of the adjustable spanner with masking tape to protect the chrome finish, then undo the headgear nut anticlockwise. The headgear lifts out of the tap body.

The washer sits on the base of the headgear, held by a small nut (the jumper nut). Undo the jumper nut with the pliers and remove the old washer. Fit the new washer of the same size and replace the jumper nut, finger-tight. Check that the new washer seats flat and is not folded or kinked. Inspect the brass seat inside the tap body for scoring or pitting: a scored seat will not seal well even with a new washer.

Smear a small amount of silicone grease on the headgear threads and reinsert the headgear into the tap body. Tighten the headgear nut with the spanner: firm, not crushing. Replace the tap cover. Turn the isolating valve or stop tap back on. Close the tap and check that the drip has stopped. Leave the tap closed for 10 minutes and check again.

Some older pillar taps have a fixed jumper rather than a removable one: the washer is peened over a fixed pin and must be cut free with a knife. Replace the whole jumper assembly (available from plumbing merchants by tap make) rather than trying to re-washer a fixed jumper. On a mixer tap with a ceramic cartridge, a drip requires cartridge replacement rather than a washer change.

A tap washer in normal domestic use lasts five to ten years. Hard water shortens washer life because the scale deposits on the brass seat scratch the washer each time the tap is closed. Fit rubber washers rather than fibre: rubber handles hard water better. A tap that drips again within a year of a washer change usually has a scored seat: regrind the seat with a tap reseating tool before fitting the next washer.

attrs.fix: Insert a tap reseating tool (a hand-turned grinding tool that fits into the tap body) and grind the seat flat. Five to ten turns is usually enough to remove the scoring. Fit a fresh washer after reseating.
attrs.cause: The brass seat inside the tap body is scored or pitted, preventing the new washer from sealing flat against it.
attrs.symptom: Tap still drips after fitting a new washer

attrs.fix: Tighten the gland nut (the smaller nut around the spindle above the headgear nut) a quarter-turn at a time until the leak stops. If tightening makes the tap stiff to turn, the gland needs repacking with PTFE tape wound around the spindle.
attrs.cause: The gland packing around the headgear spindle has worn out; the gland nut needs tightening or repacking.
attrs.symptom: Water leaks from around the headgear nut after reassembly
